sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 7, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at (22,22). RIGHT was blocked (no movement). The 6-pixel grid shows many W walls blocking most paths. Rows 34 and 40 are completely walled. Need to find alternate routes.
- Action model: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT are valid - suggests movement-based navigation.
- Recent findings: RIGHT from (22,22) blocked by wall at (22,28). Need to check DOWN and LEFT.
- Plan: Try DOWN to explore, then map out reachable positions and find path to goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 6 of about 19 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, DOWN worked. The player moved from (22, 22) to (28, 22), which is 6 pixels down. Now I have a clearer picture of the grid.
The grid has rows at: 16, 19, 22, 25, 28, 31, 34, 37, 40, 43, 46
And cols at: 16, 19, 22, 25, 28, 31, 34, 37, 40, 43, 46
The player moves in 6-pixel increments on the sub-grid: rows 16, 22, 28, 34, 40, 46 and cols 16, 22, 28, 34, 40, 46.
Let me look at the 6-pixel grid again (player positions only):
```
    16  19  22  25  28  31  34  37  40  43  46
16:  W   g   W   .   W   g   W   g   W   .   .
22:  .   .   W   .   W   g   W   g   W   g   W
28:  .   .   b   g   W   .   W   .   .   .   W
34:  W   g   W   g   W   g   W   .   W   g   W
40:  W   g   W   g   W   g   W   .   W   g   W
46:  W   g   W   .   .   .   W   g   W   .   N
```
Player positions (6-pixel grid):
```
    16  22  28  34  40  46
16:  W   W   W   W   W   .
22:  .   W   W   W   W   W
28:  .   b   W   W   .   W
34:  W   W   W   W   W   W
40:  W   W   W   W   W   W
46:  W   W   .   W   W   N
```
Player is at (28, 22). From here:
- UP: (22, 22) = W (blocked)
- DOWN: (34, 22) = W (blocked)
- LEFT: (28, 16) = . (walkable)
- RIGHT: (28, 28) = W (blocked)
So from (28, 22), only LEFT is available. Let me go LEFT to (28, 16).
From (28, 16):
- UP: (22, 16) = . (walkable)
- DOWN: (34, 16) = W (blocked)
- LEFT: out of bounds
- RIGHT: (28, 22) = walkable
From (22, 16):
- UP: (16, 16) = W (blocked)
- DOWN: (28, 16) = . (walkable)
- LEFT: out of bounds
- RIGHT: (22, 22) = W (blocked)
So from the current position, the reachable cells on the 6-pixel grid are: (28, 22), (28, 16), (22, 16). None of these lead to the goal at (46, 46).
But wait, rows 34 and 40 are all walls on the 6-pixel grid. How can the player reach row 46?
Let me reconsider the movement model. Maybe the player doesn't move in exactly 6-pixel increments. Maybe the player slides until hitting a wall, and the slide distance varies.
Let me think about this differently. When the player is at (28, 22) and presses DOWN, where would the player slide to? The player would slide down until hitting a wall. Looking at column 22:
- Row 28: player (b)
- Row 34: W (wall)
So pressing DOWN from (28, 22) would slide the player to... (34, 22)? But that's a wall. Or would the player stop just before the wall at (31, 22)?
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The player moved from (22, 22) to (28, 22) with DOWN. That's 6 pixels. Looking at column 22 in the grid:
- Row 22: was player position
- Row 25: g (gray block)
- Row 28: player is now here
- Row 31: (empty)
- Row 34: W (wall)
So the player slid from row 22 to row 28, passing through row 25 which has a g block. The player stopped at row 28. Why? Maybe because there's something at row 28 that stopped the player, or maybe the player always moves exactly 6 pixels.
But earlier, when I pressed RIGHT from (22, 22), the player didn't move at all. Looking at row 22:
- Col 22: player
- Col 25: (empty)
- Col 28: W (wall)
If the player always moves 6 pixels, RIGHT from (22, 22) would go to (22, 28), which is a wall. So the player can't move there.
But DOWN from (28, 22) would go to (34, 22), which is also a wall. So DOWN should be blocked too.
Let me test this by trying DOWN from the current position.
Wait, but the goal is at (46, 46). If rows 34 and 40 are all walls, how can the player reach row 46? There must be some way through.
Let me look more carefully at the grid. Maybe there are gaps in rows 34 and 40 that I missed.
Row 34: WgWgWgW.WgW.
- Col 16: W
- Col 19: g
- Col 22: W
- Col 25: g
- Col 28: W
- Col 31: g
- Col 34: W
- Col 37: .
- Col 40: W
- Col 43: g
- Col 46: W
Row 40: WgWgWgW.WgW.
- Col 16: W
- Col 19: g
- Col 22: W
- Col 25: g
- Col 28: W
- Col 31: g
- Col 34: W
- Col 37: .
- Col 40: W
- Col 43: g
- Col 46: W
At col 37, both rows have '.' (empty). But the player moves on the 6-pixel grid, which has cols at 16, 22, 28, 34, 40, 46. Col 37 is not on this grid.
Hmm, maybe the player CAN move to positions that are not on the 6-pixel grid. Let me reconsider the movement model.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the intermediate rows between the 6-pixel grid:
- Row 19: ..g.g...g...
- Row 25: ..g.g.g...g.
- Row 31: ......g...g.
- Row 37: g.g.........
- Row 43: ..g...g.g.g.
These rows have g blocks at various positions. Maybe the player can move to these intermediate rows as well.
Let me reconsider the movement. When the player presses DOWN, maybe the player slides down until hitting a wall (W), not just 6 pixels. The player stopped at row 28 because there's a wall or obstacle there.
Actually, I think the key is that the player slides until just BEFORE a wall. Let me trace the path:
- From (16, 16), RIGHT: player slides right. At col 19 there's a g block, at col 22 there's a W wall. The player stops at col 22 (the wall position? or just before it?).
- From (16, 22), DOWN: player slides down. At row 19 there's nothing at col 22, at row 22 there's... the player ends up at row 22.
Wait, I'm confused. Let me re-examine the movement pattern.
Initial position: blue at (16, 16), charcoal at (16, 17)
After RIGHT: blue at (16, 22), charcoal at (16, 23)
After DOWN: blue at (22, 22), charcoal at (23, 22)
After RIGHT: no movement (blocked)
After DOWN: blue at (28, 22), charcoal at (29, 22)
So the player moved:
1. (16, 16) → RIGHT → (16, 22): +6 col
2. (16, 22) → DOWN → (22, 22): +6 row
3. (22, 22) → RIGHT → blocked (would go to (22, 28) which is W)
4. (22, 22) → DOWN → (28, 22): +6 row
So the player moves in 6-pixel increments, and can only move to positions that are not walls.
Now, from (28, 22), the possible moves are:
- UP: (22, 22) = was player position, now what? Let me check.
- DOWN: (34, 22) = W (blocked)
- LEFT: (28, 16) = . (walkable)
- RIGHT: (28, 28) = W (blocked)
So from (28, 22), only UP and LEFT are available. But going UP just goes back to (22, 22), and going LEFT goes to (28, 16).
From (28, 16):
- UP: (22, 16) = . (walkable)
- DOWN: (34, 16) = W (blocked)
- LEFT: out of bounds (or blocked)
- RIGHT: (28, 22) = walkable
From (22, 16):
- UP: (16, 16) = W (blocked)
- DOWN: (28, 16) = . (walkable)
- LEFT: out of bounds
- RIGHT: (22, 22) = W (blocked)
So the reachable positions from the current state are: (28, 22), (28, 16), (22, 16), (22, 22). None of these lead to the goal at (46, 46).
This means either:
1. The 6-pixel grid model is wrong
2. There's a different movement mechanic I'm missing
3. The player needs to go through non-6-pixel positions
Let me reconsider. Maybe the player can move to intermediate positions like (31, 22) or (37, 22). Let me check if there are walls at those positions.
Looking at the grid:
- (31, 22): not in block map, so empty
- (34, 22): W (wall)
- (37, 22): not in block map, so empty
- (40, 22): W (wall)
- (43, 22): g (gray block)
- (46, 22): W (wall)
So at col 22, the walls are at rows 34, 40, 46. The player could potentially move to rows 31, 37, 43 if the movement allows it.
But the player has been moving in 6-pixel increments so far. Maybe the movement distance depends on the direction and the maze layout.
Actually, let me reconsider the slide mechanics. Maybe the player slides until hitting a wall, and the slide distance varies. Let me trace:
- From (16, 16), RIGHT: player slides right. The first wall to the right is at (16, 22). The player stops at (16, 22). But wait, (16, 22) IS a wall. So does the player stop AT the wall or BEFORE the wall?
- From (16, 22), DOWN: player slides down. The first wall below is at (22, 22)? But the player ended up at (22, 22). Hmm.
Actually, I think the player stops just BEFORE the wall. So:
- From (16, 16), RIGHT: player slides right. The wall is at (16, 22). The player stops at (16, 22 - 1) = (16, 21)? But the player ended up at (16, 22).
- Or maybe the player stops AT the wall position, replacing it.
I'm getting confused. Let me just try different moves and see what happens. From the current position (28, 22), let me try LEFT and see where the player goes.
Actually, I realize I should step back and think about this more carefully. The player is currently at (28, 22). I need to get to (46, 46). The 6-pixel grid shows that rows 34 and 40 are all walls, which would block vertical movement. But maybe the player can move to non-6-pixel positions.
Let me try a different approach: let me explore all possible moves from the current position and see where they lead. I'll try LEFT, then from there try different directions.
Actually, wait. I just realized something. The player moved from (22, 22) to (28, 22) with DOWN. But (28, 22) is NOT a wall position - it's empty (the player is there now). And (34, 22) IS a wall. So the player slid 6 pixels down and stopped at (28, 22), which is 6 pixels before the wall at (34, 22).
But if the player slides until hitting a wall, why didn't the player slide further? From (28, 22), the next wall below is at (34, 22), which is 6 pixels away. So if the player pressed DOWN again, would it slide to (34, 22)? But (34, 22) is a wall, so maybe the player can't move there.
Let me test: from (28, 22), try DOWN. If the player moves to (34, 22), that means the player can move into wall positions. If the player doesn't move, that means wall positions block movement.
Actually, I already know that from (22, 22), RIGHT was blocked because (22, 28) is a wall. So walls DO block movement. From (28, 22), DOWN should also be blocked because (34, 22) is a wall.
So the reachable positions from the current state are limited. Let me think about how to reach the goal.
Wait, maybe I need to reconsider the grid. Let me look at the full grid again:
```
    16  19  22  25  28  31  34  37  40  43  46
16:  W   g   W   .   W   g   W   g   W   .   .
19:  .   .   g   .   g   .   .   .   g   .   .
22:  .   .   W   .   W   g   W   g   W   g   W
25:  .   .   g   .   g   .   g   .   .   .   g
28:  .   .   b   g   W   .   W   .   .   .   W
29:  .   .   c   .   .   .   .   .   .   .   .
31:  .   .   .   .   .   .   g   .   .   .   g
34:  W   g   W   g   W   g   W   .   W   g   W
37:  g   .   g   .   .   .   .   .   .   .   .
40:  W   g   W   g   W   g   W   .   W   g   W
43:  .   .   g   .   .   .   g   .   g   .   g
46:  W   g   W   .   .   .   W   g   W   .   N

Hmm, I see that at col 37, rows 34 and 40 have '.' (empty). Maybe the player can move to col 37? But the 6-pixel grid has cols at 16, 22, 28, 34, 40, 46. Col 37 is not on this grid.
Wait, but the player started at col 16 and moved to col 22 (6 pixels). What if the player can also move to col 19 (3 pixels) or other intermediate positions?
Actually, I think the movement grid might be more fine-grained. Let me look at the row positions: 16, 19, 22, 25, 28, 31, 34, 37, 40, 43, 46. The gaps are: 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3. So the grid has 3-pixel spacing in both directions!
But the player moves in 6-pixel increments, which is 2 grid cells. So the player can only reach positions that are multiples of 6 from the starting position.
Starting from (16, 16):
- Rows reachable: 16, 22, 28, 34, 40, 46 (every 6 pixels)
- Cols reachable: 16, 22, 28, 34, 40, 46 (every 6 pixels)
This gives the 6x6 grid I showed earlier. And on this grid, rows 34 and 40 are all walls, blocking vertical movement.
But wait, maybe the player can move in 3-pixel increments too, not just 6. Let me check if the player ever moved 3 pixels.
From the history:
- (16, 16) → RIGHT → (16, 22): +6 col
- (16, 22) → DOWN → (22, 22): +6 row
- (22, 22) → RIGHT → blocked
- (22, 22) → DOWN → (28, 22): +6 row
All moves so far have been 6 pixels. But maybe some moves could be 3 pixels if the wall is closer.
Actually, I think the movement is: the player slides in the chosen direction until hitting a wall or the edge of the maze. The slide distance depends on where the walls are.
From (16, 16), RIGHT: the player slides right. The first wall to the right is at (16, 22). The player stops at (16, 22). Wait, but (16, 22) is a wall. So does the player stop AT the wall or just BEFORE it?
Looking at the result: the player ended up at (16, 22), which is the wall position. So the player can move TO wall positions? That doesn't make sense.
Actually, I think what happened is: the player at (16, 16) pressed RIGHT, and the player slid to (16, 22). The wall at (16, 22) was "replaced" by the player, or the player moved past it.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the walls don't block the player's movement; they're just decorative or represent something else. Let me look at the grid more carefully.
Actually, I think I've been overcomplicating this. Let me just try to navigate to the goal by exploring different paths. From the current position (28, 22), let me try LEFT and see where the player goes, then continue exploring.
Actually, I realize I should try a completely different approach. Let me use BFS to find a path, treating the movement as: from each position, the player can move UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT, and the player slides until hitting a wall (W). The player's new position is the last non-wall cell in that direction.
But I need to understand what "hitting a wall" means. Does the player stop at the wall cell, or just before it?
From the evidence:
- From (16, 16), RIGHT → (16, 22): the wall at (16, 22) is a W. The player ended up at (16, 22).
- From (16, 22), DOWN → (22, 22): what's at (22, 22)? In the original grid, (22, 22) was a W. The player ended up at (22, 22).
- From (22, 22), RIGHT → blocked: (22, 28) is a W. The player didn't move.
- From (22, 22), DOWN → (28, 22): what's at (28, 22)? In the original grid, (28, 22) was empty (not in the block map). The player ended up at (28, 22).
So the pattern seems to be:
- If the target cell (6 pixels away) is a wall, the player can still move there (if it's the first wall in that direction).
- If the target cell is empty, the player moves there.
- But from (22, 22), RIGHT was blocked because (22, 28) is a wall.
Wait, this is inconsistent. From (16, 16), RIGHT moved to (16, 22) which is a wall. From (22, 22), RIGHT was blocked because (22, 28) is a wall.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the player can only move to wall positions if they're "entering" the maze, but not if they're already inside. Or maybe there's a different rule.
Actually, I think the issue is that the player can move to a cell if it's NOT a wall, OR if it's a wall that the player hasn't visited yet. Once a wall cell is "activated" by the player, it becomes a non-wall cell for future movements.
Hmm, this is getting too speculative. Let me just try to navigate and see what happens. From (28, 22), let me try LEFT.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at (28,22). The 6-pixel grid shows rows 34 and 40 as all walls, blocking vertical progress. Need to find alternate route. The full grid has 3-pixel spacing with intermediate rows/cols.
Recent findings: Player moves in 6-pixel increments. Walls (W) block movement in some cases. Need to explore more.
Plan: Try LEFT from current position, then explore to find path through the maze.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT from (28, 22)
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'c', 'N'] and n['pixels'] <= 9:
        b = n['boundary']
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center=({cr},{cc}) px={n['pixels']}")

# Now try DOWN from new position
result2 = action(['DOWN'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'c', 'N'] and n['pixels'] <= 9:
        b = n['boundary']
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center=({cr},{cc}) px={n['pixels']}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  N: center=(46,46) px=9

result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'c', 'N'] and n['pixels'] <= 9:
        b = n['boundary']
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center=({cr},{cc}) px={n['pixels']}")

# Now try DOWN from new position

for n in seg2['nodes']:
